sourceRefs.fallbackText: If __hash__() is not explicitly defined, or if it is set to None , then @dataclass may add an implicit __hash__() method. Although not recommended, you can force @dataclass to create a __hash__() method with unsafe_hash=True . This might be the case if your class is logically immutable but can still be mutated. sourceRefs.fallbackText: This module provides a decorator and functions for automatically adding generated special methods such as __init__() and __repr__() to user-defined classes. It was originally described in PEP 557 .
sourceRefs.textQuote: This module provides a decorator and functions for automatically adding generated special methods such as __init__() and __repr__() to user-defined classes. It was originally described in PEP 557 .

highSignalBlocks.text: This module provides a decorator and functions for automatically adding generated special methods such as __init__() and __repr__() to user-defined classes. It was originally described in PEP 557 .

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The member variables to use in these generated methods are defined using PEP 526 type annotations. For example, this code:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The @dataclass decorator examines the class to find field s. A field is defined as a class variable that has a type annotation . With two exceptions described below, nothing in @dataclass examines the type specified in the variable annotation.
sourceRefs.textQuote: The @dataclass decorator examines the class to find field s. A field is defined as a class variable that has a type annotation . With two exceptions described below, nothing in @dataclass examines the type specified in t

highSignalBlocks.text: The @dataclass decorator examines the class to find field s. A field is defined as a class variable that has a type annotation . With two exceptions described below, nothing in @dataclass examines the type specified in the variable annotation.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The order of the fields in all of the generated methods is the order in which they appear in the class definition.
sourceRefs.textQuote: The order of the fields in all of the generated methods is the order in which they appear in the class definition.

highSignalBlocks.text: The order of the fields in all of the generated methods is the order in which they appear in the class definition.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The @dataclass decorator will add various “dunder” methods to the class, described below. If any of the added methods already exist in the class, the behavior depends on the parameter, as documented below. The decorator returns the same class that it is called on; no new class is created.

If any of the added methods already exist in the class, the behavior depends on the parameter, as documented below. The decorator returns the same class that it is called on; no new class is created.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: repr : If true (the default), a __repr__() method will be generated. The generated repr string will have the class name and the name and repr of each field, in the order they are defined in the class. Fields that are marked as being excluded from the repr are not included. For example: InventoryItem(name='widget', unit_price=3.0, quantity_on_hand=10) .

sourceRefs.fallbackText: order : If true (the default is False ), __lt__() , __le__() , __gt__() , and __ge__() methods will be generated. These compare the class as if it were a tuple of its fields, in order. Both instances in the comparison must be of the identical type. If order is true and eq is false, a ValueError is raised.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: unsafe_hash : If true, force dataclasses to create a __hash__() method, even though it may not be safe to do so. Otherwise, generate a __hash__() method according to how eq and frozen are set. The default value is False .
sourceRefs.textQuote: unsafe_hash : If true, force dataclasses to create a __hash__() method, even though it may not be safe to do so. Otherwise, generate a __hash__() method according to how eq and frozen are set. The default value is False

highSignalBlocks.text: unsafe_hash : If true, force dataclasses to create a __hash__() method, even though it may not be safe to do so. Otherwise, generate a __hash__() method according to how eq and frozen are set. The default value is False .

sourceRefs.fallbackText: __hash__() is used by built-in hash() , and when objects are added to hashed collections such as dictionaries and sets. Having a __hash__() implies that instances of the class are immutable. Mutability is a complicated property that depends on the programmer’s intent, the existence and behavior of __eq__() , and the values of the eq and frozen flags in the @
sourceRefs.textQuote: __hash__() is used by built-in hash() , and when objects are added to hashed collections such as dictionaries and sets. Having a __hash__() implies that instances of the class are immutable. Mutability is a complicated p

highSignalBlocks.text: __hash__() is used by built-in hash() , and when objects are added to hashed collections such as dictionaries and sets. Having a __hash__() implies that instances of the class are immutable. Mutability is a complicated property that depends on the programmer’s intent, the existence and behavior of __eq__() , and the values of the eq and frozen flags in the @dataclass decorator.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: By default, @dataclass will not implicitly add a __hash__() method unless it is safe to do so. Neither will it add or change an existing explicitly defined __hash__() method. Setting the class attribute __hash__ = None has a specific meaning to Python, as described in the __hash__() documentation.
sourceRefs.textQuote: By default, @dataclass will not implicitly add a __hash__() method unless it is safe to do so. Neither will it add or change an existing explicitly defined __hash__() method. Setting the class attribute __hash__ = None h

highSignalBlocks.text: By default, @dataclass will not implicitly add a __hash__() method unless it is safe to do so. Neither will it add or change an existing explicitly defined __hash__() method. Setting the class attribute __hash__ = None has a specific meaning to Python, as described in the __hash__() documentation.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Here are the rules governing implicit creation of a __hash__() method. Note that you cannot both have an explicit __hash__() method in your dataclass and set unsafe_hash=True ; this will result in a TypeError .
sourceRefs.textQuote: Here are the rules governing implicit creation of a __hash__() method. Note that you cannot both have an explicit __hash__() method in your dataclass and set unsafe_hash=True ; this will result in a TypeError .

highSignalBlocks.text: Here are the rules governing implicit creation of a __hash__() method. Note that you cannot both have an explicit __hash__() method in your dataclass and set unsafe_hash=True ; this will result in a TypeError .

sourceRefs.fallbackText: If eq and frozen are both true, by default @dataclass will generate a __hash__() method for you. If eq is true and frozen is false, __hash__() will be set to None , marking it unhashable (which it is, since it is mutable). If eq is false, __hash__() will be left untouched meaning the __hash__() method of the superclass will be used (if the superclass is obje
sourceRefs.textQuote: If eq and frozen are both true, by default @dataclass will generate a __hash__() method for you. If eq is true and frozen is false, __hash__() will be set to None , marking it unhashable (which it is, since it is mutable

highSignalBlocks.text: If eq and frozen are both true, by default @dataclass will generate a __hash__() method for you. If eq is true and frozen is false, __hash__() will be set to None , marking it unhashable (which it is, since it is mutable). If eq is false, __hash__() will be left untouched meaning the __hash__() method of the superclass will be used (if the superclass is object , this means it will fall back to id-based hashing).

sourceRefs.fallbackText: frozen : If true (the default is False ), assigning to fields will generate an exception. This emulates read-only frozen instances. See the discussion below.
sourceRefs.textQuote: frozen : If true (the default is False ), assigning to fields will generate an exception. This emulates read-only frozen instances. See the discussion below.

highSignalBlocks.text: frozen : If true (the default is False ), assigning to fields will generate an exception. This emulates read-only frozen instances. See the discussion below.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: If __setattr__() or __delattr__() is defined in the class and frozen is true, then TypeError is raised.

highSignalBlocks.text: If __setattr__() or __delattr__() is defined in the class and frozen is true, then TypeError is raised.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: match_args : If true (the default is True ), the __match_args__ tuple will be created from the list of non keyword-only parameters to the generated __init__() method (even if __init__() is not generated, see above). If false, or if __match_args__ is already defined in the class, then __match_args__ will not be generated.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: kw_only : If true (the default value is False ), then all fields will be marked as keyword-only. If a field is marked as keyword-only, then the only effect is that the __init__() parameter generated from a keyword-only field must be specified with a keyword when __init__() is called. See the parameter glossary entry for details. Also see the KW_ONLY section.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Changed in version 3.11: If a field name is already included in the __slots__ of a base class, it will not be included in the generated __slots__ to prevent overriding them . Therefore, do not use __slots__ to retrieve the field names of a dataclass. Use fields() instead. To be able to determine inherited slots, base class __slots__ may be any iterable, but
sourceRefs.textQuote: Changed in version 3.11: If a field name is already included in the __slots__ of a base class, it will not be included in the generated __slots__ to prevent overriding them . Therefore, do not use __slots__ to retrieve t

highSignalBlocks.text: Changed in version 3.11: If a field name is already included in the __slots__ of a base class, it will not be included in the generated __slots__ to prevent overriding them . Therefore, do not use __slots__ to retrieve the field names of a dataclass. Use fields() instead. To be able to determine inherited slots, base class __slots__ may be any iterable, but not an iterator.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: As shown above, the MISSING value is a sentinel object used to detect if some parameters are provided by the user. This sentinel is used because None is a valid value for some parameters with a distinct meaning. No code should directly use the MISSING value.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: default : If provided, this will be the default value for this field. This is needed because the field() call itself replaces the normal position of the default value.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: default_factory : If provided, it must be a zero-argument callable that will be called when a default value is needed for this field. Among other purposes, this can be used to specify fields with mutable default values, as discussed below. It is an error to specify both default and default_factory .

Among other purposes, this can be used to specify fields with mutable default values, as discussed below. It is an error to specify both default and default_factory .

sourceRefs.fallbackText: init : If true (the default), this field is included as a parameter to the generated __init__() method.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: hash : This can be a bool or None . If true, this field is included in the generated __hash__() method. If false, this field is excluded from the generated __hash__() . If None (the default), use the value of compare : this would normally be the expected behavior, since a field should be included in the hash if it’s used for comparisons. Setting this value t
sourceRefs.textQuote: hash : This can be a bool or None . If true, this field is included in the generated __hash__() method. If false, this field is excluded from the generated __hash__() . If None (the default), use the value of compare : t

highSignalBlocks.text: hash : This can be a bool or None . If true, this field is included in the generated __hash__() method. If false, this field is excluded from the generated __hash__() . If None (the default), use the value of compare : this would normally be the expected behavior, since a field should be included in the hash if it’s used for comparisons. Setting this value to anything other than None is discouraged.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: One possible reason to set hash=False but compare=True would be if a field is expensive to compute a hash value for, that field is needed for equality testing, and there are other fields that contribute to the type’s hash value. Even if a field is excluded from the hash, it will still be used for comparisons.

Even if a field is excluded from the hash, it will still be used for comparisons.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: compare : If true (the default), this field is included in the generated equality and comparison methods ( __eq__() , __gt__() , et al.).
sourceRefs.textQuote: compare : If true (the default), this field is included in the generated equality and comparison methods ( __eq__() , __gt__() , et al.).

sourceRefs.fallbackText: metadata : This can be a mapping or None . None is treated as an empty dict. This value is wrapped in MappingProxyType() to make it read-only, and exposed on the Field object. It is not used at all by Data Classes, and is provided as a third-party extension mechanism. Multiple third-parties can each have their own key, to use as a namespace in the metadata.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: kw_only : If true, this field will be marked as keyword-only. This is used when the generated __init__() method’s parameters are computed.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: If the default value of a field is specified by a call to field() , then the class attribute for this field will be replaced by the specified default value. If default is not provided, then the class attribute will be deleted. The intent is that after the @dataclass decorator runs, the class attributes will all contain the default values for the fields, just
sourceRefs.textQuote: If the default value of a field is specified by a call to field() , then the class attribute for this field will be replaced by the specified default value. If default is not provided, then the class attribute will be de

highSignalBlocks.text: If the default value of a field is specified by a call to field() , then the class attribute for this field will be replaced by the specified default value. If default is not provided, then the class attribute will be deleted. The intent is that after the @dataclass decorator runs, the class attributes will all contain the default values for the fields, just as if the default value itself were specified. For example, after:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: @dataclass class C : x : int y : int = field ( repr = False ) z : int = field ( repr = False , default = 10 ) t : int = 20
sourceRefs.textQuote: @dataclass class C : x : int y : int = field ( repr = False ) z : int = field ( repr = False , default = 10 ) t : int = 20

highSignalBlocks.text: @dataclass class C : x : int y : int = field ( repr = False ) z : int = field ( repr = False , default = 10 ) t : int = 20

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The class attribute C.z will be 10 , the class attribute C.t will be 20 , and the class attributes C.x and C.y will not be set.
sourceRefs.textQuote: The class attribute C.z will be 10 , the class attribute C.t will be 20 , and the class attributes C.x and C.y will not be set.

highSignalBlocks.text: The class attribute C.z will be 10 , the class attribute C.t will be 20 , and the class attributes C.x and C.y will not be set.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: InitVar[T] type annotations describe variables that are init-only . sourceRefs.fallbackText: Returns a tuple of Field objects that define the fields for this dataclass. Accepts either a dataclass, or an instance of a dataclass. Raises TypeError if not passed a dataclass or instance of one. Does not return pseudo-fields which are ClassVar or InitVar .

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Converts the dataclass obj to a dict (by using the factory function dict_factory ). Each dataclass is converted to a dict of its fields, as name: value pairs. dataclasses, dicts, lists, and tuples are recursed into. Other objects are copied with copy.deepcopy() .

highSignalBlocks.text: When defined on the class, it will be called by the generated __init__() , normally as self.__post_init__() . However, if any InitVar fields are defined, they will also be passed to __post_init__() in the order they were defined in the class. If no __init__() method is generated, then __post_init__() will not automatically be called.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Among other uses, this allows for initializing field values that depend on one or more other fields. For example:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The __init__() method generated by @dataclass does not call base class __init__() methods. If the base class has an __init__() method that has to be called, it is common to call this method in a __post_init__() method:
sourceRefs.textQuote: The __init__() method generated by @dataclass does not call base class __init__() methods. If the base class has an __init__() method that has to be called, it is common to call this method in a __post_init__() method:

highSignalBlocks.text: The __init__() method generated by @dataclass does not call base class __init__() methods. If the base class has an __init__() method that has to be called, it is common to call this method in a __post_init__() method:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: class Rectangle : def __init__ ( self , height , width ): self . height = height self . width = width @dataclass class Square ( Rectangle ): side : float def __post_init__ ( self ): super () . __init__ ( self . side , self . side )
sourceRefs.textQuote: class Rectangle : def __init__ ( self , height , width ): self . height = height self . width = width @dataclass class Square ( Rectangle ): side : float def __post_init__ ( self ): super () . __init__ ( self . side , se

highSignalBlocks.text: class Rectangle : def __init__ ( self , height , width ): self . height = height self . width = width @dataclass class Square ( Rectangle ): side : float def __post_init__ ( self ): super () . __init__ ( self . side , self . side )

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Note, however, that in general the dataclass-generated __init__() methods don’t need to be called, since the derived dataclass will take care of initializing all fields of any base class that is a dataclass itself.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Note, however, that in general the dataclass-generated __init__() methods don’t need to be called, since the derived dataclass will take care of initializing all fields of any base class that is a dataclass itself.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: See the section below on init-only variables for ways to pass parameters to __post_init__() . Also see the warning about how replace() handles init=False fields.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: One of the few places where @dataclass actually inspects the type of a field is to determine if a field is a class variable as defined in PEP 526 . It does this by checking if the type of the field is typing.ClassVar . If a field is a ClassVar , it is excluded from consideration as a field and is ignored by the dataclass mechanisms. Such ClassVar pseudo-fiel
sourceRefs.textQuote: One of the few places where @dataclass actually inspects the type of a field is to determine if a field is a class variable as defined in PEP 526 . It does this by checking if the type of the field is typing.ClassVar . I

highSignalBlocks.text: One of the few places where @dataclass actually inspects the type of a field is to determine if a field is a class variable as defined in PEP 526 . It does this by checking if the type of the field is typing.ClassVar . If a field is a ClassVar , it is excluded from consideration as a field and is ignored by the dataclass mechanisms. Such ClassVar pseudo-fields are not returned by the module-level fields() function.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Another place where @dataclass inspects a type annotation is to determine if a field is an init-only variable. It does this by seeing if the type of a field is of type InitVar . If a field is an InitVar , it is considered a pseudo-field called an init-only field. As it is not a true field, it is not returned by the module-level fields() function. Init-only f
sourceRefs.textQuote: Another place where @dataclass inspects a type annotation is to determine if a field is an init-only variable. It does this by seeing if the type of a field is of type InitVar . If a field is an InitVar , it is considere

highSignalBlocks.text: Another place where @dataclass inspects a type annotation is to determine if a field is an init-only variable. It does this by seeing if the type of a field is of type InitVar . If a field is an InitVar , it is considered a pseudo-field called an init-only field. As it is not a true field, it is not returned by the module-level fields() function. Init-only fields are added as parameters to the generated __init__() method, and are passed to the optional __post_init__() method. They are not otherwise used by dataclasses.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: For example, suppose a field will be initialized from a database, if a value is not provided when creating the class:
sourceRefs.textQuote: For example, suppose a field will be initialized from a database, if a value is not provided when creating the class:

highSignalBlocks.text: For example, suppose a field will be initialized from a database, if a value is not provided when creating the class:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: @dataclass class C : i : int j : int | None = None database : InitVar [ DatabaseType | None ] = None def __post_init__ ( self , database ): if self . j is None and database is not None : self . j = database . lookup ( 'j' ) c = C ( 10 , database = my_database )
sourceRefs.textQuote: @dataclass class C : i : int j : int | None = None database : InitVar [ DatabaseType | None ] = None def __post_init__ ( self , database ): if self . j is None and database is not None : self . j = database . lookup ( 'j

highSignalBlocks.text: @dataclass class C : i : int j : int | None = None database : InitVar [ DatabaseType | None ] = None def __post_init__ ( self , database ): if self . j is None and database is not None : self . j = database . lookup ( 'j' ) c = C ( 10 , database = my_database )

sourceRefs.fallbackText: In this case, fields() will return Field objects for i and j , but not for database .
sourceRefs.textQuote: In this case, fields() will return Field objects for i and j , but not for database .

highSignalBlocks.text: In this case, fields() will return Field objects for i and j , but not for database .

sourceRefs.fallbackText: It is not possible to create truly immutable Python objects. However, by passing frozen=True to the @dataclass decorator you can emulate immutability. In that case, dataclasses will add __setattr__() and __delattr__() methods to the class. These methods will raise a FrozenInstanceError when invoked.
sourceRefs.textQuote: It is not possible to create truly immutable Python objects. However, by passing frozen=True to the @dataclass decorator you can emulate immutability. In that case, dataclasses will add __setattr__() and __delattr__() me

highSignalBlocks.text: It is not possible to create truly immutable Python objects. However, by passing frozen=True to the @dataclass decorator you can emulate immutability. In that case, dataclasses will add __setattr__() and __delattr__() methods to the class. These methods will raise a FrozenInstanceError when invoked.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: There is a tiny performance penalty when using frozen=True : __init__() cannot use simple assignment to initialize fields, and must use object.__setattr__() .
sourceRefs.textQuote: There is a tiny performance penalty when using frozen=True : __init__() cannot use simple assignment to initialize fields, and must use object.__setattr__() .

highSignalBlocks.text: There is a tiny performance penalty when using frozen=True : __init__() cannot use simple assignment to initialize fields, and must use object.__setattr__() .

sourceRefs.fallbackText: When the dataclass is being created by the @dataclass decorator, it looks through all of the class’s base classes in reverse MRO (that is, starting at object ) and, for each dataclass that it finds, adds the fields from that base class to an ordered mapping of fields. After all of the base class fields are added, it adds its own fields to the ordered mapping
sourceRefs.textQuote: When the dataclass is being created by the @dataclass decorator, it looks through all of the class’s base classes in reverse MRO (that is, starting at object ) and, for each dataclass that it finds, adds the fields from

highSignalBlocks.text: When the dataclass is being created by the @dataclass decorator, it looks through all of the class’s base classes in reverse MRO (that is, starting at object ) and, for each dataclass that it finds, adds the fields from that base class to an ordered mapping of fields. After all of the base class fields are added, it adds its own fields to the ordered mapping. All of the generated methods will use this combined, calculated ordered mapping of fields. Because the fields are in insertion order, derived classes override base classes. An example:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: @dataclass class Base : x : Any = 15.0 y : int = 0 @dataclass class C ( Base ): z : int = 10 x : int = 15
sourceRefs.textQuote: @dataclass class Base : x : Any = 15.0 y : int = 0 @dataclass class C ( Base ): z : int = 10 x : int = 15

highSignalBlocks.text: @dataclass class Base : x : Any = 15.0 y : int = 0 @dataclass class C ( Base ): z : int = 10 x : int = 15

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The final list of fields is, in order, x , y , z . The final type of x is int , as specified in class C .
sourceRefs.textQuote: The final list of fields is, in order, x , y , z . The final type of x is int , as specified in class C .

highSignalBlocks.text: The final list of fields is, in order, x , y , z . The final type of x is int , as specified in class C .

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The generated __init__() method for C will look like:
sourceRefs.textQuote: The generated __init__() method for C will look like:

highSignalBlocks.text: The generated __init__() method for C will look like:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: def __init__ ( self , x : int = 15 , y : int = 0 , z : int = 10 ):
sourceRefs.textQuote: def __init__ ( self , x : int = 15 , y : int = 0 , z : int = 10 ):

highSignalBlocks.text: def __init__ ( self , x : int = 15 , y : int = 0 , z : int = 10 ):

sourceRefs.fallbackText: After the parameters needed for __init__() are computed, any keyword-only parameters are moved to come after all regular (non-keyword-only) parameters. This is a requirement of how keyword-only parameters are implemented in Python: they must come after non-keyword-only parameters.
sourceRefs.textQuote: After the parameters needed for __init__() are computed, any keyword-only parameters are moved to come after all regular (non-keyword-only) parameters. This is a requirement of how keyword-only parameters are implemented

highSignalBlocks.text: After the parameters needed for __init__() are computed, any keyword-only parameters are moved to come after all regular (non-keyword-only) parameters. This is a requirement of how keyword-only parameters are implemented in Python: they must come after non-keyword-only parameters.
